elements.sent-2411-001: We present a series of equations that track the total realized and unrealized profits and losses at any time, incorporating the spread.
elements.sent-2411-002: The resulting formalism is ideally suited to evaluate the performance of trading model algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-003: Finance and economics are characterized by a high level of formal abstraction, akin to the level of mathematization seen in physics.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is thus no surprise to encounter monographs entitled The Statistical Mechanics of Financial Markets or Physics and Finance \citep{voit2005statistical,ziemann2021physics .}
elements.sent-2411-005: Indeed, this analytical sophistication has sometimes been criticized for its lack of empirical justification and validation \citep{bouchaud2008economics,labini2016science .}
elements.sent-2411-006: In stark contrast, the practitioners of finance appear to face a seemingly simple challenge when assessing performance measures associated with their trading activity.
elements.sent-2411-007: After all, only basic arithmetic operations are required, alongside the calculation of percentages.
elements.sent-2411-008: As a result, these topics are mostly glossed over in standard textbooks on finance \citep{hull2000options , where spot trading is often covered only tangentially \citep{chan2013algorithmic , or reduced to measures relating to the net asset value and mark-to-market valuations.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Nonetheless, calculating the profits and losses (PnL) from any trading activity is a multifaceted task that can be approached in various ways, each with its own set of assumptions, advantages, and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, some quantitative analysts use logarithmic mid prices to compute returns \citep[p.
elements.sent-2411-011: Choices must be made regarding the handling of fees, the utilization of compounded calculations, and whether PnL is realized or remains unrealized.
elements.sent-2411-012: In the following, we outline a straightforward methodology addressing these topics in detail.
elements.sent-2411-013: Given a currency pair, the first currency is called the base currency ($\mathtt{b}$) and the second one is the quote currency ($\mathtt{q}$).
elements.sent-2411-014: The price at time $t_i$ is defined as $x_i$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We follow a balance-sheet bookkeeping approach.
elements.sent-2411-016: As an example, the actions of buying $u_1$ units of a currency pair at $t_1$ and then $u_2$ units at $t_2$ is represented as.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{c|c} $\mathtt{b}$ $\mathtt{q}$ $\mathtt{b}_1 = u_1$ $\mathtt{q}_1 = - x_1 u_1 $ $\mathtt{b}_2 = \mathtt{b}_1 + u_2$ $\mathtt{q}_2 = \mathtt{q}_1 - x_2 u_2$ \dots \dots \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-017: Selling $u_i > 0$ units of a currency pair would be accounted for as $\mathtt{b _i = \mathtt{b _{i-1 -u_i$ and $\mathtt{q _i = \mathtt{q _{i-1 + u_i x_i$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In summary \begin{subequations \begin{equation}b_i = \sum_{j=1}^i u_j, q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^i x_j u_j.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus, $x_i$ will be assumed to be the bid or ask price according to the trading context and $x_i^\prime$ will denote the opposite of the bid/ask pair and the spread is given by $s_i = |x_i - x^\prime_i|$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Having set out the basics, the unrealized average price at time $t_i$ is given by \begin{equation}\bar x_i = -\frac{\mathtt{q}_i}{\mathtt{b}_i}. \label{eq:av}\end{equation} Utilizing this calculated price the PnL can be evaluated at any given time.
elements.sent-2411-021: Note that even when $\mathtt{b _i$ goes to zero, $\mathtt{q _i$ has the realized PnL accounted for, yielding the total calculation at all times.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In general, the PnL expressed in $\mathtt{b}$ units at time $t_i$ is given by \begin{equation}p^\mathtt{b}_i= \begin{cases} \mathtt{b}_i (1 - \frac{\bar x_i}{x_i^\prime}), \text{if } \mathtt{b}_i \neq 0, -u_i (1 - \frac{\bar x_{i-1} + \mu}{\hat x_i}), \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} \label{eq:pnl}\end{equation} where if \begingroup \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.5 \setlength{\tabcolsep {12pt.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}p^b_i=  b_i (1 - \frac{\bar x_i}{x_i^\prime}), if  b_i \neq 0, -u_i (1 - \frac{\bar x_{i-1} + \mu}{\hat x_i}), otherwise,  \label{eq:pnl}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{l||c|c|} $\mathtt{b}_{i-1} > 0$ $\mathtt{b}_{i-1} < 0$ $\mathtt{q}_i > 0$ $\mu = s_i$, \; $\hat x_i = x_i^\prime$ $\mu = 0$, \; $\hat x_i = x_i$ $\mathtt{q}_i < 0$ $\mu = 0$, \; $\hat x_i = x_i$ $\mu = -s_i$, \; $\hat x_i = x_i^\prime$ \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-023: Note that if mid prices $x_i^\dag$ are used in the computation, the expression simplifies, as $\hat x_i = x_i = x_i^\prime = x_i^\dag$ and $s_i = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-024: To illustrate, consider the following trades for the pair SOL/USDT.
elements.sent-2411-025: Observe that the profit of USDT 260 can be sold for SOL at the ask price of 170.25 resulting in a profit of SOL $1.527165\dots$, as Eq. (\ref{eq:pnl ) correctly computes.}
elements.sent-2411-026: For any currency pair, the base currency is the anchor for the PnL computation.
elements.sent-2411-027: Moreover, $p^\mathtt{b _i$ computes the total of the PnL at time $t_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, to retrieve the individual trade returns in base units the differences are caclculated \begin{equation}\delta p^\mathtt{b}_i = p^\mathtt{b}_i - p^\mathtt{b}_{i-i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-029: The PnL in quote units at time $t_i$ is given by $p^\mathtt{q _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In order to evaluate this expression the opposite trade prices $x_i^\prime$ are utilized \begin{subequations \begin{equation}p^q_i = p^b_i x_i^\prime, \label{eq:pnlqa} \delta p^q_i = p^q_i - p^q_{i-i}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-034: As a final remark, if trading fees are charged, depending on if they are denoted in the base or quote currency, they need to be deducted from $\delta p^\mathtt{b _i$ or $\delta p^\mathtt{q _i$, respectively.}}

elements.sent-2411-053: Now assume you have an initial base balance of $\mathcal{B = 500$ and in addition an initial quote balance of $\mathcal{Q = 75'000$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our total wealth $\mathcal{W}$ is defined as the sum of the two balances, split up equally\footnote{The PnL computations of this section do not depend on this assumption.}

elements.sent-2411-056: In detail, the initial wealth evaluated at the price $x_i$ is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\overline W^b_i := B +Q^{b} := B + \frac{Q}{x_i^\prime}, \overline W^q_i := Q + B^{\, q} := Q + x_i^\prime B.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\overline W^b_i := B +Q^{b} := B + \frac{Q}{x_i^\prime}, \overline W^q_i := Q + B^{\, q} := Q + x_i^\prime B.\end{equation}
